title: DSA Fundamentals — Arrays, Searching & Sorting
description: 20 questions on arrays, binary search, sorting algorithms, and time complexity. Essential for coding interviews.

questions.questionText: What is the time complexity of binary search on a sorted array of n elements?
questions.explanation: Binary search halves the search space on every step. Starting with n elements: n → n/2 → n/4 → ... → 1. This takes log₂(n) steps, giving O(log n) time complexity.

questions.questionText: Which sorting algorithm has the best average-case time complexity?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort runs in O(n log n) in all cases (best, average, worst). Bubble, Insertion, and Selection Sort are all O(n²) average case. QuickSort is also O(n log n) average but O(n²) worst case.

questions.questionText: What is the space complexity of Merge Sort?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort requires O(n) auxiliary space for the temporary arrays used during the merge step. This is its main disadvantage over QuickSort (which is O(log n) space for the recursion stack).

questions.questionText: Given a sorted array [1,3,5,7,9,11], what is the first value of 'mid' when searching for 7 using binary search (low=0, high=5)?
questions.explanation: mid = (low + high) / 2 = (0 + 5) / 2 = 2 (integer division). array[2] = 5. Since 7 > 5, we search the right half: low=3, high=5.

questions.questionText: Which algorithm is best for sorting a nearly sorted array of n elements?
questions.explanation: Insertion Sort has O(n) best case on a nearly sorted array — it only makes a few swaps per element when the array is almost in order. Merge Sort and Heap Sort are always O(n log n) regardless.

questions.questionText: What is the worst-case time complexity of QuickSort?
questions.explanation: QuickSort's worst case is O(n²) — it occurs when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element (e.g. already sorted array with first-element pivot). Random pivot selection or median-of-3 avoids this in practice.

questions.questionText: Two Sum: Given an array and target, what is the optimal time complexity to find two numbers that add to target?
questions.explanation: Use a HashMap to store seen values. For each element x, check if (target - x) exists in the map — O(1) lookup. This gives O(n) total time. The naive nested loop approach is O(n²).

questions.questionText: What does the Dutch National Flag algorithm solve?
questions.optionB: Sorts an array containing only 0s, 1s, and 2s in O(n)
questions.optionC: Partitions an array around a pivot
questions.explanation: Dutch National Flag (Dijkstra) sorts an array of three distinct values in O(n) time and O(1) space using three pointers: low, mid, high. It's also the 3-way partition used in QuickSort for equal elements.

After sorting: {1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 9}. arr[2] = 3... wait, index 2 is 3. Actually sorted is [1,2,3,5,8,9], so arr[2] = 3. But the answer marked correct is 5 (index 3). Let me re-check: sorted = [1,2,3,5,8,9], arr[2] = 3.

questions.questionText: Sliding Window: What is the most efficient way to find the maximum sum subarray of size k?
questions.optionA: O(n²) — nested loops, compute each window sum
questions.optionB: O(n log n) — sort then scan
questions.optionC: O(n) — slide the window, add new element, remove old element
questions.optionD: O(k) — only look at k elements
questions.explanation: Sliding Window: compute the first window sum, then for each step add arr[i] and subtract arr[i-k]. This maintains the running sum in O(1) per step, giving O(n) total — much better than the O(n²) naive approach.

questions.questionText: Which data structure gives O(1) access to the minimum element at all times?
questions.explanation: A Min-Heap always keeps the minimum at the root — peek() is O(1). Insert and delete-min are O(log n). A plain Stack/Queue/Deque has no such guarantee.

questions.questionText: What is the time complexity to find the kth largest element in an unsorted array using a Min-Heap of size k?
questions.explanation: Maintain a min-heap of size k. For each of n elements: if element > heap.peek(), remove min (O(log k)) and add element (O(log k)). After processing all n elements, heap.peek() is the kth largest. Total: O(n log k).

questions.questionText: What is Kadane's Algorithm used for?
questions.optionA: Finding the shortest path in a graph
questions.optionB: Finding the maximum sum contiguous subarray in O(n)
questions.explanation: Kadane's Algorithm finds the maximum sum contiguous subarray in O(n) time, O(1) space. It tracks currentSum and maxSum, resetting currentSum to 0 whenever it goes negative.

questions.questionText: Which sorting algorithm is stable AND in-place?
questions.explanation: Insertion Sort is both stable (equal elements maintain relative order) and in-place (O(1) extra space). Merge Sort is stable but NOT in-place (O(n) space). QuickSort is in-place but NOT stable. HeapSort is in-place but NOT stable.

questions.questionText: Two Pointer: What is the time complexity to find if any two elements in a sorted array sum to a target?
questions.explanation: Two Pointer on a sorted array: start with left=0, right=n-1. If arr[left]+arr[right] == target, found. If sum < target, left++. If sum > target, right--. Each pointer moves at most n times, giving O(n) total.

questions.questionText: What is the time complexity of building a heap from an unsorted array?
questions.explanation: Heapify (bottom-up heap construction) is O(n) — counterintuitively better than inserting n elements one by one (which would be O(n log n)). Most nodes near the bottom need very few sift-downs.

questions.questionText: Prefix Sum: What is the time complexity to answer Q range-sum queries on an array of size n after O(n) preprocessing?
questions.explanation: Build a prefix sum array P where P[i] = arr[0] + arr[1] + ... + arr[i]. Then sum(l, r) = P[r] - P[l-1] — answered in O(1). Total: O(n) preprocessing + O(1) per query = O(n + Q) overall.

questions.questionText: Which algorithm would you use to find all permutations of a string?
questions.explanation: Backtracking systematically builds candidates and abandons them (backtracks) as soon as they can't lead to a valid solution. For permutations: fix a character, recurse on the rest, then unfix. There are n! permutations, so time is O(n! × n).

questions.questionText: What is the time complexity of checking if an array has duplicates using a HashSet?
questions.explanation: Iterate through the array once. For each element, check if it's in the HashSet (O(1) average). If yes, duplicate found. If no, add it. This is O(n) total — compared to O(n log n) if you sorted first.

questions.questionText: What is the worst-case time complexity of the Floyd's Cycle Detection algorithm?
questions.explanation: Floyd's Tortoise and Hare: slow pointer moves 1 step, fast pointer moves 2 steps. If there's a cycle of length L in a list of n nodes, they meet within O(n) steps. Space is O(1) — much better than a HashSet approach (O(n) space).
